canada_economic_profile.top_global_ai_clusters_presence: no Canadian cluster in top 25 globally

cluster_analysis.methodology: K-Means clustering on Talent, Infrastructure, Operating Environment, Research, Development, Government Strategy, Commercial Ecosystem, Scale, Intensity

scenario_1_rd.name: R&D Focus: Translational R&D Mission + Sovereign Compute + Mitacs Talent

scenario_2_government.name: Government Focus: Policy Coordination + Strategic Support

peer_comparisons.USA: leads through scale, deep capital markets, and widespread deployment
peer_comparisons.China: leads through industrial integration and state-supported scaling
peer_comparisons.EU: benefits from larger coordinated markets and stronger industrial clusters
peer_comparisons.Canada: strong supply (talent, research, startups) but weak demand-side adoption and firm-level execution

key_findings: Canada has a credible AI economy: 140K+ professionals, 670+ AI startups, $8.6B VC in 2022
Canada ranks 5th on Stanford AI Vibrancy Index and 5th of 62 countries for AI capacity
Business AI adoption at only 12% — significantly below international peers
45% of Canadian AI firms cite high compute costs as the top growth barrier
Canada has no AI cluster in the global top 25 — lags US, China, EU in commercialization hubs
Canada lags all G7 nations in HPC infrastructure — essential for scaling AI to products
Patent growth is strong in absolute terms but commercialization gap persists
Canada has the ingredients of a strong AI economy but not the demand-side adoption or scaled deployment of leading nations
Scenario analysis shows largest gains achievable through combined R&D + government + economic interventions
core_gap: Canada has strong AI supply (talent, startups, research) but consistently underperforms in converting this into widespread business adoption, scaled deployment, and productivity gains. The execution gap between research-excellence and commercial outcomes is the defining challenge.
